title: Egg on Carpet
timeEstimate: 15–30 minutes; cold water rule is the whole protocol
stainChemistry: Egg is concentrated albumin (egg white) + yolk (which adds fat + protein). Raw egg is water-soluble and easy to remove IF treated cold. Hot or warm water instantly COOKS the albumin into an insoluble white solid that bonds mechanically to carpet fiber. Cooked / heat-set egg is a permanent plastic-like film. The #1 mistake: someone pours warm water on the spill to "rinse it off," and instead cooks the egg into the carpet. Cold water ONLY is the entire rule for this stain, even if the egg was warm when it landed.
whyThisWorks: Cold water keeps the albumin mobile so it can be blotted / rinsed out rather than cooked in. Enzyme pretreat in WARM (not hot) water then digests any remaining albumin without setting it. Dawn handles the fat fraction from the yolk. Skipping cold-first or using hot water at any step locks in the protein permanently — no home chemistry reverses a cooked egg in carpet.
defaultAssumption: Breakfast spill — dropped raw egg, cracked while cooking. Fresh + still liquid = very winnable. Dried overnight = harder, but the protein isn't heat-set unless someone used hot water on it. If someone DID use warm/hot water on it before you got there, the egg is likely cooked into the carpet permanently.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scoop any yolk / shell pieces into a garbage bag with a spoon. Press paper towels STRAIGHT DOWN on the liquid. Lift straight up. Repeat with fresh towels until no more transfers. Don't rub.

spottingProtocol.agent: COLD Water Blot — No Warm, No Hot
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cloth, dry towel + weight
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pour a SMALL amount of COLD water on the spot. Blot with dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 passes. Cold only — warm / hot water immediately cooks egg white into a permanent plastic film. Don't flood — pad wick.
Dawn in cold water on damp cloth. Work in with soft toothbrush 5 min. Lifts yolk fat and most remaining albumin. Blot. Damp-cloth rinse — NEVER pour water.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz in WARM (<100°F) water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Enzyme pretreat in WARM water — bath temperature, NOT hot. Damp cloth, press 15 min. Digests any remaining albumin. Don't use hot — even this late-stage hot would still set residual.
Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan-dry. Inspect in daylight. Residual film = already heat-set (someone used warm water) = pro, not more home rounds.

steps: 2. COLD water small-volume dilute, blot between.
3. Dawn + cold water damp cloth + soft brush.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through initial; warm (<100°F) for enzyme; NEVER hot
warnings: NEVER warm or hot water — cooks albumin permanently
Cooked egg is not removable by home chemistry — pro only
Cold-water-only rule even if egg was hot when it landed

homeSolutions.agent: COLD Water Only — This Is the Whole Rule
homeSolutions.instruction: Egg on carpet is easy IF you use cold water. It's permanent IF you use warm or hot water. That's the whole fight. Egg white (albumin) cooks into an insoluble plastic film the moment it hits heat — same chemistry as frying an egg. If someone has already poured warm water on the spot, the egg is likely cooked in and a pro is the only option. If you caught it before any heat, you're in great shape. Even if the egg was warm when it landed (just cracked, still from a warm pan), use COLD water now — the stain itself is still mobile if it hasn't been cooked AGAIN.

Use a spoon to scoop up any yolk or shell into the trash. Press paper towels STRAIGHT DOWN on the liquid. Lift straight up, press fresh ones, repeat. Don't rub — rubbing drives protein and fat into the pad.

Pour a SMALL amount of COLD tap water on the spot and blot with a dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 times. Cold only. Don't use warm, don't use hot, don't use room temp if your tap runs warm. If unsure, run the cold tap for 30 seconds before filling a cup. Don't flood — water that reaches the pad is trapped there.

Mix 1 tablespoon Dawn in 2 cups cold water. Dampen a white cloth, work into the spot with a soft toothbrush for 5 minutes. Dawn handles the fat from the yolk and lifts most of the remaining albumin. Damp-cloth rinse — don't pour water.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat in WARM (Not Hot) Water
homeSolutions.instruction: Dissolve OxiClean Versatile or Biz in WARM water — bath temperature, under 100°F. NOT hot. Warm is needed for enzyme activity but wouldn't cook already-weakened albumin the way hot would. Dampen a white cloth in the solution, press on the spot, cover with a damp towel, 15 minutes. Digests any residual egg.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, 30 minutes. Fan-dry. Inspect in daylight. Fully clean = win. White / yellowish film remaining = already heat-set (warm water was used at some point) — home chemistry can't reverse that. Pro.

If the egg got warm / hot water before you started, or if a white film remains after the full cold-water cycle: pro. Tell them: "Egg on carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with cold water + Dawn + enzyme (warm, not hot). [No hot water used / warm water was used before I got there]. [Residual white film / fully clear]." Be honest about whether warm water was used — it changes what's possible.

steps: 1. COLD WATER ONLY. Warm = cooked = permanent.
3. Cold water small-volume dilute, blot between, 2–3 passes.
4. Dawn + cold water damp cloth + soft brush, 5 min.
5. Enzyme pretreat WARM (<100°F, NOT hot), 15 min.
7. Heat-set = pro (cold water only can't reverse cooked albumin).
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if warm or hot water was used on the spot before you got there (egg likely cooked in), or if a white film remains after cold-water cycle.
warnings: NEVER warm or hot water — cooks albumin permanently
Cooked egg in carpet is pro-only
Cold water only on initial blotting

neverDo: Never warm or hot water on raw egg
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / olefin carpet: tolerates full protocol
Wool: pH-sensitive — hidden test on enzyme
Most carpet dyes: stable against cold + Dawn + enzyme

materialWarnings: Cold water only — warm water = cooked egg = permanent
Heat at any step sets the protein

escalation.when: Warm / hot water was used on the spot before treatment, white albumin film remains after cold-water cycle, or stain dried overnight with heat exposure.
escalation.whatToTell: "Egg on carpet, age [fresh / overnight], home-treated with cold water + Dawn + enzyme (warm, not hot). [No hot water / warm water was used before I got here]. [Residual white film / fully clear]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with protein / cooked-albumin chemistry

contextQuestions: Raw egg (dropped from carton) or cooked egg (scrambled / fried)?
Has anyone used warm / hot water on the spot?
Whole egg or just white / just yolk?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Cooked egg (scrambled, fried) dropped on carpet is simpler — no albumin-cooking risk, just scoop + blot + Dawn for any fat. Raw egg is the hard variant because of the cold-water rule. The warm-water-first mistake is extremely common and irreversible.
